critic_raw:
This experiment evaluating RNN with attention vs. logistic regression for patient readmission prediction shares several structural problems with similar cases in this batch. The stratified random split assumes 'temporal autocorrelation is sufficiently weak' — but for 100,000 admissions across three years from a multi-site hospital system, this is a strong and unjustified assumption. Hospital admission patterns are seasonal, subject to policy changes, and diagnostic coding practice evolves. Random splitting allows future admission patterns to appear in training data.

Recall is selected as the primary metric 'to identify as many true readmissions as possible,' but using recall alone as the optimization target is dangerous — it incentivizes models to predict positive for all patients, achieving 100% recall with 0% precision. A precision-recall curve with operating-point selection, or PR-AUC, would be more informative. The design should at minimum specify a minimum precision threshold below which high recall is considered unacceptable for clinical deployment.

The design mentions including 'demographic and hospital identifiers within the feature set' to mitigate confounds — but including hospital identifiers as features is not the same as controlling for hospital effects. If model A performs better on hospital X and model B on hospital Y, a global metric will obscure this. Hospital-stratified evaluation, or ideally leave-hospital-out evaluation, is needed. Including hospital ID as a feature can also encode protected attribute proxies that affect fairness.

The comparison gives the RNN access to 'sequential interactions within patient data' while logistic regression uses a 'grid search optimized' model on demographic and clinical features. The feature sets are not equalized, which confounds architecture with feature richness — a recurring problem in this experiment family.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on multi-year, multi-site admission data ignores temporal and institutional autocorrelation, risking leakage of future care patterns into training.
Recall-only primary metric creates a pathological incentive to predict all patients as readmission-positive; PR-AUC or recall at specified precision thresholds is the appropriate metric.
Including hospital identifiers as model features is not equivalent to controlling for hospital effects — hospital-stratified or leave-hospital-out evaluation is needed.
Feature set asymmetry between RNN (sequential interactions) and logistic regression (demographic and clinical aggregates) confounds architecture comparison with feature richness.
No calibration analysis is included — in clinical settings the model's probability outputs drive intervention prioritization, making calibration at least as important as rank-ordering.
Temporal autocorrelation in readmission risk is asserted as weak without empirical validation
Recall as the sole primary metric without a precision constraint is operationally uninterpretable and selects toward trivially positive-biased models
Hospital identifiers as input features encode site-specific base rates that do not generalize to new deployment sites
Attention mechanism type and implementation are unspecified, making the architectural advantage claim non-verifiable
No patient-level split containment is described for multi-admission patients across the three-year EHR window

Stratified random split on three-year hospital readmission data does not account for temporal non-stationarity including COVID-period discontinuities; chronological splitting with patient-level blocking is required
Recall as the primary optimization criterion for model selection enables a degenerate all-positive classifier to score optimally; F1 or AUROC should be the validation selection criterion

Comparison confounds model class (RNN vs. logistic regression) with feature representation (sequential admission data vs. summarized statistics); no intermediate baseline isolates the sequential modeling contribution

Hospital identifier used as a feature rather than a stratification variable conflates confound control with prediction, and may harm generalization to unseen hospitals while introducing demographic proxy effects

No patient-level grouping specified in the split strategy — patients with multiple admissions across the three-year window may appear in both training and test, inflating performance estimates through direct leakage
